file_name: Papachristou et al. - 2024 - Evaluation of an artificial intelligence-based decision support for detection of cutaneous melanoma.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study evaluated an AI-based clinical decision support tool (Dermalyser) using a machine learning algorithm trained on dermoscopic images to detect cutaneous melanoma in primary care. The AI system processed dermoscopic images via a smartphone app to provide diagnostic guidance, and its performance metrics (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity) were calculated based on these AI-generated outputs compared against histopathological diagnoses.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is titled 'Evaluation of an artificial intelligence-based decision support for the detection of cutaneous melanoma in primary care: a prospective real-life clinical trial.' The abstract explicitly states it is a 'prospective multicentre clinical trial' conducted at 36 primary care centers in Sweden. It reports original experimental results, including diagnostic performance metrics (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity) derived from 253 lesions in 228 patients, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Evaluation of an artificial intelligence-based decision support for the detection of cutaneous melanoma in primary care: a prospective real-life clinical trial
brief_summary: This prospective multicentre clinical trial evaluated the diagnostic performance of Dermalyser, an AI-based clinical decision support tool for detecting cutaneous melanoma in primary care. Conducted across 36 primary care centers in Sweden, the study involved 228 patients with 253 skin lesions of concern, where primary care physicians used a smartphone app to analyze dermoscopic images. The AI system provided dichotomous guidance on melanoma suspicion, which was compared against histopathological diagnoses obtained via surgical excision or dermatologist referral. The app demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy with an AUROC of 0.960 for all melanomas and 0.988 for invasive melanomas alone, achieving a negative predictive value of 99.5%. The study concludes that this AI decision support tool shows high accuracy and may help primary care physicians avoid unnecessary excisions of benign lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study. Included 21 lesions total: 11 invasive melanomas (all superficial spreading type) and 10 melanomas in situ. The AI tool was evaluated for its ability to detect these against non-melanoma lesions.

The comparator group consisting of 221 lesions diagnosed as non-melanoma. Specific subtypes included: Nondysplastic melanocytic naevus (66), Seborrhoeic keratosis (69), Dysplastic melanocytic naevus (30), Congenital melanocytic naevus (11), Dermatofibroma (8), BCC (11), Haemangioma (7), SCC (incl. in situ) (4), Solar lentigo (8), Actinic keratosis (2), and Other (13).

Mentioned in the exclusion criteria ('any other cutaneous malignancy (such as basal cell or squamous cell carcinoma) were not included') and listed in Table 1 as a diagnosis for 11 lesions in the nonmelanoma group. Note: The text states lesions suspected of being BCC were excluded, but Table 1 lists BCC diagnoses, implying some may have been included if initial suspicion was melanoma or the exclusion applied to the recruitment phase for specific high-suspicion BCC cases. However, the primary focus is melanoma vs non-melanoma.

all_task_types.details: The AI tool (Dermalyser) processes dermoscopic images to provide a dichotomous decision support text regarding evidence for melanoma. The algorithm produces a value between 0 and 1 corresponding to a calculated probability of the lesion being a melanoma, which is then used to classify the lesion as either 'evidence of melanoma detected' or 'no evidence of melanoma detected' based on a predefined cutoff. The primary evaluation metric is the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) curve for differentiating melanomas from nonmelanomas.

datasets_used.name: Prospective Clinical Trial Cohort (Dermalyser Study)
datasets_used.description: A prospective multicentre clinical trial dataset collected from 36 primary care centers in Sweden between May and December 2022. It consists of dermoscopic images and clinical data from 228 patients with 253 skin lesions of concern, assessed by primary care physicians using a smartphone app (Dermalyser). The dataset includes final diagnoses based on histopathology or dermatologist clinical assessment.

use.testing: Used as the primary evaluation cohort to determine the diagnostic performance (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, NPV) of the AI-based decision support tool.

datasets_used.name: In Silico Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopic images provided by Aigora GmbH used for the convolutional neural network training prior to the clinical trial. The specific size, source, or class distribution of this dataset is not reported in the text.

use.training: Used for training the convolutional neural network underlying the Dermalyser app.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUROC) curve

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of the AI app in differentiating melanomas from nonmelanomas and invasive melanomas from other lesions.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) curve
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify the overall diagnostic performance of the AI app.
To estimate the uncertainty around the AUROC values for all melanomas and invasive melanomas.
To determine the maximum diagnostic performance of the app at the best-performing point on the ROC curve and at the predefined cutoff level.

statistical_methods_used.method: Positive Predictive Value (PPV) and Negative Predictive Value (NPV) calculation
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the clinical utility of the app's guidance at the predefined cutoff level and when combined with physician suspicion.
To describe the number of lesions suspected to be melanoma that need to undergo histopathological investigation to detect at least one case, based on the app's guidance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Diagnostic performance was evaluated using Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) curves and 95% Confidence Intervals (CI), but no statistical hypothesis tests comparing groups or against a null value were reported.

Prospective Clinical Trial Cohort (Derma.metric: AUROC, Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV, NNI
Prospective Clinical Trial Cohort (Derma.notes: The study included 253 lesions in 228 patients. 21 lesions were melanomas (11 invasive, 10 in situ). The app missed one in situ melanoma. The app's guidance was the strongest predictor of melanoma when adjusted for clinical variables (OR 26.55, 95% CI 3.29–213.96, p=0.002). PCPs reported their suspicion would have increased for 61 lesions (24.1%) and decreased for 56 lesions (22.1%) if they had followed the app's guidance. 140 lesions (55.3%) might not have needed further assessment if the app's guidance had been followed.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained in silico on an extensive number of dermoscopic images of skin lesions prior to the study start; algorithm was fixed prior to data collection with no further training or modification during the study.

architecture_details.details: The specific architecture name, layer count, or backbone is not reported. The algorithm produces a value between 0 and 1 corresponding to a calculated probability of the lesion being a melanoma.

training_details.dataset: In silico dataset of dermoscopic images provided by Aigora GmbH (specific size and distribution not reported)

classification_or_output_head.output: Value between 0 and 1 representing the probability of melanoma, converted to dichotomous guidance ('evidence of melanoma detected' or 'no evidence of melanoma detected') based on a predefined cutoff.

conclusions: The AI-based clinical decision support tool (Dermalyser) demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy for detecting cutaneous melanoma when used prospectively by primary care physicians in a real-life clinical setting.
The tool achieved an AUROC of 0.960 for all melanomas and 0.988 for invasive melanomas, with a negative predictive value of 99.5% for all melanomas and 100% for invasive melanomas.
The app correctly identified all invasive melanomas and all but one in situ melanoma.

The study findings suggest that this decision support tool may help primary care physicians avoid unnecessary surgical excision of benign skin lesions, potentially reducing the demand for dermatologist and pathologist assessment.
The app's guidance was the strongest predictor of melanoma when adjusted for clinical variables such as lesion size, patient age, and sex.
The study population was predominantly Fitzpatrick skin types I–II, and the low proportion of pT2 or higher melanomas limits the generalizability of the results to other populations or lesion stages.
Further research, preferably with a randomized study design, is warranted to determine the tool’s actual usefulness and diagnostic safety over time.
important_remarks: The study population was predominantly Fitzpatrick skin types I–II, with an explicit exclusion of patients with skin types V–VI, limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations.
The proportion of pT2 or higher melanomas was low (only thin invasive melanomas and in situ melanomas were included), which may limit applicability to thicker melanomas.
Not all benign lesions were histopathologically confirmed; some were diagnosed by dermatologist assessment, which mirrors standard Swedish primary care but introduces a reference standard limitation.
The algorithm was fixed prior to data collection with no further training or modification during the study, preventing data leakage but also preventing adaptation to the specific clinical trial data.

The primary care physician (PCP) was instructed not to let the app guidance affect their clinical management, meaning the study evaluated diagnostic accuracy rather than the impact of AI-guided decision-making on patient outcomes.

There was varying inclusion rate among the 36 participating primary care centers, with some centers contributing no lesions, reflecting real-world challenges but potentially introducing selection bias.
Data addressing PCP characteristics, such as clinical experience or workplace staffing conditions, was absent, which may be relevant for interpreting the results.
The study lacked a randomized design; the authors note that further research with a randomized study design is warranted to determine the tool's actual usefulness and diagnostic safety over time.
The app missed one in situ melanoma, resulting in a negative predictive value of 99.5% for all melanomas and 100% for invasive melanomas.

The study was funded by Region Östergötland and the AIDA network, and one author (P.P.) is a co-founder of the clinical decision support tool (Dermalyser) studied, indicating a potential conflict of interest.
